sections.section_title: In a rush? Take it with you!
sections.text: On-the-GO! (OTG) is one of your carry-out options for dining on campus. Located adjacent to Earhart, Ford and Windsor Dining Courts as well as our premiere Academic OTG location in Lawson Computer Science Building. OTG provides a variety of cold sandwiches and salads, sushi and snacks and is an added convenience for students who don't have time to stop and sit down for a meal. Meal Swipes are accepted at all On-the-GO! locations.

sections.section_title: Residential On-the-GO! options available at all residential locations for one swipe of your PUID = 1 meal:
sections.text: 1 Entree + 2 sides + Fountain Drink, coffee or hot chocolate = 1 Meal Swipe 1 Sushi Box + Fountain Drink, coffee or hot chocolate = 1 Meal Swipe 4 sides + Fountain Drink, coffee or hot chocolate = 1 Meal Swipe

Please click on each of the following locations for more information such as contact info., address and map. Please note all OTGs are meal swipe only locations.
Ford Dining Court and OTG Earhart Dining Court and OTG Windsor Dining Court and OTG.
Please see all Dining hours of operation here.

sections.section_title: On-the-GO! at Lawson Computer Science Building:
sections.text:
On-the-GO! at Lawson Computer Science Building is our premiere OTG location with meal swipe options as well as an additional retail menu. Visit the OTG @ Lawson page here for specific hours, meal offerings and menu information.
All On-the-GO!'s are open to Purdue students with meal swipes.

Entrée + 1 Side + 1 Beverage = 1 Meal Swipe
Breakfast and Lunch: Entrée (Pick One) OTG Box Any Sandwich or Wrap Muffin Choice of Side Beverage (Pick One) Fountain Beverage Regular Coffee
No substitutions. Valid during specific meal times.

Location 305 N University Street West Lafayette, IN 47907
Email Josh Johnson john3642@purdue.edu
Phone (765) 496-3473
Most Credit Cards Meal Swipe

Meal plans for West Lafayette students living in University Residences provide many options to choose from. Whether you are always on the go, looking for more Dining Dollars to enjoy retail options or want to eat in a dining court for every meal, we have six options to choose from. With a variety of rates, options are available to fit your budget.
Unlimited All you care to eat (Access every 30 minutes during operating hours) $260 Dining Dollars per Semester 8 On-the-Go!, Quick Bite, pop-ups, or dining court carry-out swipes per Week 14-Track Ideal for students who eat two meals per day. 14 Meals per Week $440 Dining Dollars per Semester 10-Track The plan with the greatest flexibility. 10 Meals per Week $570 Dining Dollars per Semester 7-Track Ideal for students who like to eat on and off campus. 7 Meals per Week $310 Dining Dollars per Semester 80-Block For URBA residents and commuters. 80 Meals per Semester $260 Dining Dollars per Semester 50-Block For URBA residents and commuters. 50 Meals per Semester $260 Dining Dollars per Semester

All you care to eat (Access every 30 minutes during operating hours)
$260 Dining Dollars per Semester 8 On-the-Go!, Quick Bite, pop-ups, or dining court carry-out swipes per Week

Ideal for students who eat two meals per day.
14 Meals per Week $440 Dining Dollars per Semester

The plan with the greatest flexibility.
10 Meals per Week $570 Dining Dollars per Semester

Ideal for students who like to eat on and off campus.
7 Meals per Week $310 Dining Dollars per Semester

For URBA residents and commuters.
80 Meals per Semester $260 Dining Dollars per Semester

For URBA residents and commuters.
50 Meals per Semester $260 Dining Dollars per Semester

Read more about each of our plans to discover which meal plan is the best fit for your lifestyle and budget.
Unlimited This is for the student who likes to eat three full meals per day, has a preference to eat each meal in the dining courts, doesn't want to track weekly swipes used and wants the flexibility to join friends whenever and wherever they choose. It is a great option for a first-year student who is navigating their first time away from home and wants security and simplicity. The Unlimited Meal Plan affords students the most value, as it has the lowest cost per meal. With this plan, students can: Use meal swipes for breakfast, lunch, and dinner at residential dining courts any day of the week. Swipe into a dining court for an all-you-care-to-eat meal at any time. Use up to 8 meal swipes per week at On-the-GO!, Quick Bite, pop-up swipe locations, or dining court carry-out meals. Spend $260 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. 14-Track This is a great dining plan for out-of-state students who are likely to stay on campus most weekends and want the flexibility to eat a meal or two over the weekend. This is also a great plan for first-year students as it gives them the security of eating up to twice a day in an all-you-care-to-eat dining court while providing Dining Dollars to explore the many retail dining options available. The 14-Track Meal Plan is ideal for students who eat two meals per day. With this plan, students can: Use 14 meal swipes per week at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. Spend $440 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. 10-Track Designed for the student who is always on the go and will eat most of their meals in the dining courts but wants the flexibility of Dining Dollars for the option and ease of eating at retail establishments in the core of campus. The 10-Track Meal Plan offers the greatest flexibility to choose among dining court and retail locations at your convenience, with the highest amount of Dining Dollars in any of our plans. With this plan, students can: Use 10 meal swipes per week at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. Spend $570 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. 7-Track This dining plan appeals to students who may only eat one or two meals per day. This is often the plan that upperclassmen and URBA residents choose as it provides on-campus options along with the flexibility to cook at home. The 7-Track Meal Plan is ideal for students who like to eat on and off campus. With this plan, students can: Use 7 meal swipes per week, which can be used at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. Spend $310 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. 80-Block The 80-Block Meal Plan is available for residents in University Residences Boiler Apartments (URBA) and a limited number of commuter students who previously lived in University Residences. With this plan, students can: Use 80 meal swipes per semester at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. This averages out to around 5 meal swipes per week. Note that unused meal swipes expire at the end of each semester. Spend $260 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. 50-Block The 50-Block Meal Plan is available for residents in University Residences Boiler Apartments (URBA) and a limited number of commuter students who previously lived in University Residences. With this plan, students can: Use 50 meal swipes per semester at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. This averages out to around 3 meal swipes per week. Note that unused meal swipes expire at the end of each semester. Spend $260 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts.

This is for the student who likes to eat three full meals per day, has a preference to eat each meal in the dining courts, doesn't want to track weekly swipes used and wants the flexibility to join friends whenever and wherever they choose. It is a great option for a first-year student who is navigating their first time away from home and wants security and simplicity. The Unlimited Meal Plan affords students the most value, as it has the lowest cost per meal. The 14-Track Meal Plan is ideal for students who eat two meals per day. With this plan, students can: Use 14 meal swipes per week at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. Spend $440 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts.

Designed for the student who is always on the go and will eat most of their meals in the dining courts but wants the flexibility of Dining Dollars for the option and ease of eating at retail establishments in the core of campus. The 10-Track Meal Plan offers the greatest flexibility to choose among dining court and retail locations at your convenience, with the highest amount of Dining Dollars in any of our plans. With this plan, students can: Use 10 meal swipes per week at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. Spend $570 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts.

This dining plan appeals to students who may only eat one or two meals per day. This is often the plan that upperclassmen and URBA residents choose as it provides on-campus options along with the flexibility to cook at home. The 7-Track Meal Plan is ideal for students who like to eat on and off campus. With this plan, students can: Use 7 meal swipes per week, which can be used at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. Spend $310 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts.

The 80-Block Meal Plan is available for residents in University Residences Boiler Apartments (URBA) and a limited number of commuter students who previously lived in University Residences. With this plan, students can: Use 80 meal swipes per semester at five residential dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations, three Quick Bite locations and dining court carry-out meals. This averages out to around 5 meal swipes per week. Note that unused meal swipes expire at the end of each semester. This averages out to around 3 meal swipes per week. Note that unused meal swipes expire at the end of each semester. Spend $260 Dining Dollars per semester at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts.
See a side-by-side comparison of our meal plan features below. * Students have been automatically assigned a 14-track meal plan and can make their final meal plan selection during summer 2025.

sections.section_title: The Fine Print for 2025 - 2026 in West Lafayette
sections.text:
Residents of Hawkins Hall and Hilltop Apartments may choose to purchase a Purdue Dining & Culinary meal plan. Residents of these locations who select a meal plan may not change to a room-only option during the term of this contract. All University Residence Apartments, except for Hilltop Apartments, must select a meal plan . During Thanksgiving Break, Winter Break, Spring Break, and other stated University vacations, no residential meal service will be provided.
Changes to meal plans may be made to your account through the housing portal during designated dates.
Please note that meal plan prices are per academic year.

Purdue University has partnered with IU Indianapolis Dining to serve Purdue students in Indianapolis. Meal plans are available to Purdue students through IU Indianapolis Dining . Whether you are living in North Hall or are commuting to campus, click here to view available plans to you.
Don’t forget to download the Dine on Campus Mobile App!
20 block meal plan details
Sales for the Spring semester begin January 12 at 9:00 am through the housing portal Purchased once per semester 20 meal swipes per semester Meal swipes are accepted at the 5 dining courts, 4 On-the-Gos, and 3 Quick Bites locations Meal swipes are added to the student's Purdue ID within 2 business days and $258 is billed later through the bursar to the student account
With over 30 retail dining locations on campus, a Retail Dining Membership is a great way to get the most out of campus-wide dining. A membership offers the convenience of paying with your Purdue ID card and can be used at any Purdue Food Co. campus retail location . To become a member today, visit the Purdue Food Co. website .
Hungry at Purdue Indianapolis?
Get Your 10-Meal Block Plan!
For only $130 , purchase a block of 10 meals/swipes to use at:
Tower Dining – is the main dining hall with options ranging from make-your-own stir fry and salad bar to burgers and dessert. The Den @ Tower
Enjoy convenient dining options on campus without breaking the bank!
Interested? Email mealplans@purdue.edu today to get more details about purchasing this 10-block meal plan.
See a side-by-side comparison of our meal plan features below. * Students have been automatically assigned a 14-track meal plan and can make their final meal plan selection during summer 2026.

Feature Unlimited 14-Track 10-Track 7-Track 80-Block 50-Block Meals/week Access every 30 minutes * 14 10 7 - - On-the-Go!, Quick Bite, pop-ups, dining court carry-out/week 8 Combined Combined Combined Combined Combined Dining court carry-out per period (breakfast, lunch, and dinner) 1 1 1 1 1 1 Meals/semester - - - - 80 50 Dining Dollars/academic Year $535 $905 $1,175 $640 $535 $535 Available to all residential students Yes Yes Yes Yes No No UR Boiler Apartments residents Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Available to a limited number of commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences No No No No Yes Yes Price/academic year $6,072 $5,696 $5,042 $3,560 $2,524 $1,814 *during operating hours
Have questions? See our FAQs or contact mealplans@purdue.edu if you have additional questions

pages.title: Request a Meal Plan Change in West Lafayette

sections.text:
For the 2025 - 2026 Academic Year, students were preassigned the 14-track meal plan.  The Change Your Meal Plan option was available for students to make changes to their meal plan for the Fall 2025 semester from July 17 at 8:00 am ET to Friday, September 5, 2025 at 11:59 p.m., ET.
Spring 2026 Meal plan changes
The Change Your Meal Plan option was available in the housing contract portal to make changes to meal plans for the Spring 2026 semester from Tuesday, January 6 at 9:00 am ET through Friday, January 23, 2026 at 11:59 p.m., ET.  After the spring meal plans began (January 12), students were permitted to change their meal plan one time until January 23.  For meal plan changes between 1/12 and 1/23, students were charged for the number of days they have each meal plan.
After January 23, students may not make changes to their spring meal plan (raise or lower).

If you want more meals, you can move up to the Unlimited meal plan by January 23rd.  The difference in cost will be billed to your MyPurdue account. If you want more dining dollars, you can change to the 10-Track meal plan by January 23rd.  The difference in cost will be billed to your MyPurdue account. If you live in an URBA (University Residences Boiler Apartment) and wish to change from a traditional meal plan to a Block Meal plan, you can do so until January 23rd.  Any meal swipes used from the traditional meal plan will be removed from the block of swipes in the new meal plan and the difference in cost will be billed to your MyPurdue account. If you are changing plans at semester break and moving up or down into a new plan, any unspent Dining Dollars will roll over to the new plan.

pages.title: About Dining Dollars in West Lafayette

sections.section_title: What are Dining Dollars and where can I use them?
sections.text: Students can use Dining Dollars to buy snacks, beverages, convenience store items or extra meals in any of the residential dining courts or retail operations. They are automatically part of meal plans and come loaded on a student's Purdue ID card. Dining Dollars can be used at our cafés, restaurant locations and markets throughout academic campus.

What plan should I use? You should choose the dining plan that best matches how you prefer to eat. For example, if you like two to three main meals a day with some snacking in between, our Unlimited plan offers the best value. What is included in the meal plans? The Unlimited Plan affords students the most value, as it has the lowest cost per meal. This is for the student who likes to eat three full meals per day, prefers to eat each meal in the dining court, doesn’t want to track weekly swipes used, and wants the flexibility to eat whenever and wherever they choose. It is an excellent option for a first-year student navigating their first time away from home and enjoying the security and simplicity that the Unlimited offers. Suppose you don’t have enough time to eat in a dining court. You also can take eight swipes per week for any On-the-Go, Quick Bite, or dining court carry out meal. This plan offers $500 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the Dining Courts. The 14-Track Meal Plan includes 14 meals per week in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and a Dining Court Carry-out meal. This is a great dining plan for Out-of-State students likely to be around most weekends who want to have the flexibility to eat a meal or two over the weekend. It is also an excellent plan for first-year students as it gives them the security of eating twice a day each week in any residential dining location but also provides a large amount of Dining Dollars so that they can explore the many options in retail. This plan offers $850 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. The 10-Track Meal Plan includes ten meals per week in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and a dining court carry-out meal. It is designed for the student who is always on the go, will eat most meals in residential dining locations, but wants the flexibility of Dining Dollars as they spend a significant time in the core of campus and want the option and ease of eating in retail. This plan provides $1100 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across  campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. The 7-Track Meal Plan includes seven meals per week in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go areas, 3 Quick Bite locations, and dining court carry-out meals. This dining plan appeals to students who may only eat one to two meals daily and offers $600 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. This plan is often the plan that upper-classmen and URBA residents choose as it allows options and the flexibility to cook at home. The meal plans run Monday through Sunday each week of the semester. All meal plans reset Sunday night for the following week and unused meals will be forfeited. The following plans are available for residents in University Residences Boiler Apartments (URBA) including Aspire and a limited number of commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences. The 80 Block meal plan includes 80 meals per semester and can be used in any residential dining location, which include five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and dining court carry-out meals. This plan also offers $250 Dining Dollars per semester to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. This plan is available for residents in University Residence Boiler Apartments including Aspire and a limited number of commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences. The 50 Block meal plan includes 50 meals per semester and can be used in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and dining court carry-out meals. This plan also offers $250 Dining Dollars per semester to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. This plan is available for residents in University Residence Boiler Apartments including Aspire and a limited number of commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences. Dining Dollars for all meal plans are distributed by semester, half in the fall and half in the spring. Unused Dining Dollars will carry over from fall to spring semester and will expire when the meal contract ends after finals week in May. Any unused block meals at the end of each semester will be forfeited. What locations accept the 80/50 Block Plan? Meal swipes may be used in any of the residential dining locations, which include five dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations and three Quick Bite locations. The daily limit is five meal swipes (meals) per day. What is the meal swipe limit per day for the 14/10/7 Track and 80/50 Block Plans? The daily limit is five meal swipes (meals) per day. Can I change my mind? For the 2025-26 Academic Year, students were preassigned the 14-track meal plan.  Students may change their meal plan during the following dates. Please log into the Housing Portal to make any changes. The portal to make meal plan changes for Fall 2025 was open July 17 through September 5 and is now closed. The portal to make meal plan changes for Spring 2026 was open from Tuesday, January 6 at 9:00 am ET through Friday, January 23, 2026 at 11:59 p.m., ET. Spring Meal Plans begin January 12, 2026. Students could change their spring meal plan one time from January 12 through January 23 (after spring meal plans begin). For meal plan changes between 1/12 and 1/23, students were charged for the number of days they have each meal plan. For students changing from a traditional meal plan to a block meal plan between 1/12 and 1/23, any meal swipes used from the traditional meal plan will be removed from the block of swipes in the new meal plan and the meal plans charged accordingly. The last day to change to room-only (Hawkins and Hilltop) was Thursday, August 21, 2025 at 11:59 pm ET. The Fine Print Residents of Hawkins Hall and Hilltop Apartments may choose to purchase a Purdue Dining & Culinary meal plan. Residents of these locations who select a meal plan may not change to a room-only option during the term of this contract. Room-only selection must be made before the start of the contract. All University Residence Apartments, except for Hilltop Apartments, must select a meal plan. During Thanksgiving Break, Winter Break, Spring Break, and other stated University vacations, no residential meal service will be provided. Changes to meal plans may be made to your account through the housing portal during designated dates. Will retail locations accept meal swipes? No. Dining Dollar values have been increased in every meal plan and are accepted at any dining court, Purdue Food Co., or Chick-fil-A location on campus. If you are interested in dining at Purdue Food Co. and Chick-fil-A locations on campus more often, Purdue Food Co. offers retail dining memberships . Please note that these memberships must be purchased separately from your meal plan. What are Dining Dollars? Dining Dollars are yours to use at any dining court, Purdue Food Co. or Chick-fil-A location on campus. A fixed amount of dining dollars is included in every meal plan to give you additional flexibility to eat what and where you want. Dining Dollars for all meal plans are distributed by semester, half in the fall and half in the spring. Unused Dining Dollars will carry over from fall to spring semester and will expire when the meal contract ends after finals week in May. How does my meal plan work? Eating on-campus is easy with any Purdue Dining & Culinary Meal Plans. We have five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, and 3 Quick Bite locations. Once you have a meal plan, your student I.D. works like a debit card. Swipe your card, tap your phone, and the meal swipe or dining dollars are automatically deducted from your meal plan account. Our meal plans have two components: Meal Swipe and Dining Dollars. Meal Swipe: The number of swipes you have available each week is based on your selected meal plan. Meal swipes can be used in any of the five dining courts: Hillenbrand dining court, Earhart dining court, Windsor dining court, Wiley dining court, and Ford dining court, 4 On-the-Go locations: Earhart On-the-Go, Windsor On-the-Go, Ford On-the-Go, and Lawson On-the-Go, and 3 Quick Bite locations: 1 Bowl and Sushi Boss @ Meredith Hall, and Pete's Za @ Tarkington Hall. The cashier will deduct one meal from your plan upon entering these locations. You can also take your Meal to Go from our five dining courts, informing the cashier when you enter the dining court. Unused meal swipes will expire each Sunday and the meal plans will reset for the following week. Dining Dollars: This feature is accepted at all five dining courts, Chick-fil-A, and Purdue Food Co. locations. Dining Dollars work like a debit card; each time you purchase, the total is deducted from the balance in your account. Guests: Bringing your parents or a friend to a dining court?  Meal swipes are non-transferable (cannot be used for guests).  Dining dollars can be used to bring guests to the dining courts.  Dining Courts also accept Visa, MasterCard, and American Express. ResidentialDining/GuestMealPrices What happens if I find a transaction error in my account? To view your dining transactions, including Meal Swipe and Dining Dollars balances, visit eAccounts . If you find an error on a transaction, please send an email to mealplans@purdue.edu within two days so we can investigate the issue and make any corrections needed. When sending your email, please be sure to include the following information: Student’s name and university e-mail address. Description of the transaction in question and explain as clearly as possible the discrepancy. Transaction type (Meal Swipe or Dining Dollars) including the amount. How does dine-in versus carry-out in the dining courts work? Dine-in: Enjoy all-you-care-to-eat while inside the dining court. Only one piece of handheld fruit is allowed to be carried out. You may not leave the dining court with any additional food. If you would like to eat in the dining court and then get carry-out, you must exit the dining court, return to the end of the line, and re-enter to use another meal swipe to get a carry-out box. Carry-out: Students are allowed 1 carry-out per meal period (breakfast, lunch, and dinner). Unlimited meal plan holders are limited to 8 OTG!, Quick Bite, or dining court carry-out swipes per week. Students must notify the door checker that they are getting carry-out instead of dining in before they tap or swipe their PUID. Carry-out containers and to-go cups will be provided by the dining court. Carry-out container lids must be securely closed. No overloading of containers will be allowed. In addition to your carry-out container, you may carry out: 1 hot or cold beverage in a cup. (Please do not place other items inside beverage cup.) 1 whole piece of fruit or hand-held dessert. You may not dine in or sit in the dining court with a carry-out container. Violators will be reported and will face disciplinary action. How do I store my carry-out food safely? You want to avoid allowing your food to stay between 40°F and 140°F degrees which is commonly referred to as the Temperature Danger Zone. Keeping food out of the danger zone will ensure it’s safe to eat by limiting the growth of bacteria. This range - between 40 °F and 140 °F - is where bacteria is the happiest and most comfortable, and where it can grow rapidly. Four hours is the maximum time that ready-to-eat foods can stay in the temperature danger zone. After the 4 hour limit, foods must be thrown away. Ways to avoid the Danger Zone: Get what you need and eat what you get. Eat your carry-out food within two hours if you are not keeping it in a warmer or a refrigerator. Keep in mind that it is easier to keep cold food cold than to cool food off. Just because you put food into a refrigerator doesn’t mean it immediately out of the Danger Zone, especially in a hall room fridge. The time that your food takes to cool down counts towards the 2 hour limit. Consider visiting an OTG! Location. We cooled it down for you giving you more flexibility. Sites like the USDA have additional information on food safety guidelines. The more you know the better! What if my card doesn't work? Email mealplans@purdue.edu for help. If after hours, you will be contacted the next business day. For issues with Mobile ID, contact ID Card Operations at purdueidcardoffice@purdue.edu . Will Purdue be able to meet my special dietary needs? We offer a wide variety of items in our dining courts to accommodate special dietary needs. Please visit the Food Allergies information page or the Special Dietary Needs information page for specific details about managing these diets. Students are able to request a dietary accommodation if they do not feel they can successfully self-navigate in the dining courts. Students can begin this process by contacting the Disability Resource Center and disclosing their dietary needs and concerns. Please contact our registered dietitian by emailing allergicboiler@purdue.edu if you have questions about dining on campus with a special diet. What forms of payment do the dining courts accept and how much does a meal cost for a guest? The dining courts accept Dining Dollars, Visa, MasterCard and American Express. For information on pricing for guests, please visit our Guest Meal Prices webpage . Are there opportunities for employment? We are always looking for students who would like to work in a fun, fast-paced environment. We offer flexible schedules and convenient locations. As an added bonus, you receive a free meal with each shift you work. You can contact any dining court directly or follow this link to fill out an application online. What commuter meal plans are available to students who do not live in University Residences? Purdue Dining & Culinary Spring Meal Plan enrollment for commuter students will open January 12, 2026 on a limited, first-come, first-served basis. These plans are available to commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences. Any unused block meals at the end of each semester will be forfeited.  Unused dining dollars from the fall semester will carry over from the fall semester to the spring semester and will expire when the meal contract ends after finals week in May 2026. 2025 - 2026 Commuter Meal Plans: 80 Block/$260 Dining Dollars per semester (limited number of plans available, price per semester:  $1225) 50 Block/$260 Dining Dollars per semester (limited number of plans available, price per semester:  $880) More information about meal plans can be found on the meal plans page . Purdue provides a limited number of available plans to eligible commuter students every semester. Eligible students can purchase a commuter meal plan through the housing portal during designated periods. Spring 2026 Commuter Meal Plan sales will open January 12, 2026 at 9:00 am. Retail Dining Membership With over 30 retail dining locations on campus, a Retail Dining Membership is a great way to get the most out of campus-wide dining. A membership offers the convenience of paying with your Purdue ID and can be used at any Purdue Food Co. campus retail location . To become a member today, visit the Purdue Food Co. website .

You should choose the dining plan that best matches how you prefer to eat. For example, if you like two to three main meals a day with some snacking in between, our Unlimited plan offers the best value.

sections.section_title: What is included in the meal plans?
sections.text:
The Unlimited Plan affords students the most value, as it has the lowest cost per meal. This is for the student who likes to eat three full meals per day, prefers to eat each meal in the dining court, doesn’t want to track weekly swipes used, and wants the flexibility to eat whenever and wherever they choose. It is an excellent option for a first-year student navigating their first time away from home and enjoying the security and simplicity that the Unlimited offers. Suppose you don’t have enough time to eat in a dining court. You also can take eight swipes per week for any On-the-Go, Quick Bite, or dining court carry out meal. This plan offers $500 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the Dining Courts. The 14-Track Meal Plan includes 14 meals per week in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and a Dining Court Carry-out meal. This is a great dining plan for Out-of-State students likely to be around most weekends who want to have the flexibility to eat a meal or two over the weekend. It is also an excellent plan for first-year students as it gives them the security of eating twice a day each week in any residential dining location but also provides a large amount of Dining Dollars so that they can explore the many options in retail. This plan offers $850 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. The 10-Track Meal Plan includes ten meals per week in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and a dining court carry-out meal. It is designed for the student who is always on the go, will eat most meals in residential dining locations, but wants the flexibility of Dining Dollars as they spend a significant time in the core of campus and want the option and ease of eating in retail. This plan provides $1100 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across  campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. The 7-Track Meal Plan includes seven meals per week in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go areas, 3 Quick Bite locations, and dining court carry-out meals. This dining plan appeals to students who may only eat one to two meals daily and offers $600 Dining Dollars per year to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. This plan is often the plan that upper-classmen and URBA residents choose as it allows options and the flexibility to cook at home.
The meal plans run Monday through Sunday each week of the semester. All meal plans reset Sunday night for the following week and unused meals will be forfeited.
The following plans are available for residents in University Residences Boiler Apartments (URBA) including Aspire and a limited number of commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences.
The 80 Block meal plan includes 80 meals per semester and can be used in any residential dining location, which include five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and dining court carry-out meals. This plan also offers $250 Dining Dollars per semester to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. This plan is available for residents in University Residence Boiler Apartments including Aspire and a limited number of commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences. The 50 Block meal plan includes 50 meals per semester and can be used in any residential dining location, which includes five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, 3 Quick Bite locations, and dining court carry-out meals. This plan also offers $250 Dining Dollars per semester to spend at Purdue Food Co. locations across campus, Chick-fil-A, or in the dining courts. This plan is available for residents in University Residence Boiler Apartments including Aspire and a limited number of commuter students who have previously lived in University Residences.
Dining Dollars for all meal plans are distributed by semester, half in the fall and half in the spring. Unused Dining Dollars will carry over from fall to spring semester and will expire when the meal contract ends after finals week in May.
Any unused block meals at the end of each semester will be forfeited.

sections.section_title: What locations accept the 80/50 Block Plan?
sections.text: Meal swipes may be used in any of the residential dining locations, which include five dining courts, four On-the-GO! locations and three Quick Bite locations. The daily limit is five meal swipes (meals) per day.

sections.section_title: What is the meal swipe limit per day for the 14/10/7 Track and 80/50 Block Plans?
sections.text: The daily limit is five meal swipes (meals) per day.

For the 2025-26 Academic Year, students were preassigned the 14-track meal plan.  Students may change their meal plan during the following dates. Please log into the Housing Portal to make any changes.
The portal to make meal plan changes for Fall 2025 was open July 17 through September 5 and is now closed. The portal to make meal plan changes for Spring 2026 was open from Tuesday, January 6 at 9:00 am ET through Friday, January 23, 2026 at 11:59 p.m., ET. Spring Meal Plans begin January 12, 2026. Students could change their spring meal plan one time from January 12 through January 23 (after spring meal plans begin). For meal plan changes between 1/12 and 1/23, students were charged for the number of days they have each meal plan. For students changing from a traditional meal plan to a block meal plan between 1/12 and 1/23, any meal swipes used from the traditional meal plan will be removed from the block of swipes in the new meal plan and the meal plans charged accordingly. The last day to change to room-only (Hawkins and Hilltop) was Thursday, August 21, 2025 at 11:59 pm ET.
Residents of Hawkins Hall and Hilltop Apartments may choose to purchase a Purdue Dining & Culinary meal plan. Residents of these locations who select a meal plan may not change to a room-only option during the term of this contract. Room-only selection must be made before the start of the contract. All University Residence Apartments, except for Hilltop Apartments, must select a meal plan. During Thanksgiving Break, Winter Break, Spring Break, and other stated University vacations, no residential meal service will be provided.
Changes to meal plans may be made to your account through the housing portal during designated dates.

sections.section_title: Will retail locations accept meal swipes?
sections.text: No. Dining Dollar values have been increased in every meal plan and are accepted at any dining court, Purdue Food Co., or Chick-fil-A location on campus. If you are interested in dining at Purdue Food Co. and Chick-fil-A locations on campus more often, Purdue Food Co. offers retail dining memberships . Please note that these memberships must be purchased separately from your meal plan.

Dining Dollars are yours to use at any dining court, Purdue Food Co. or Chick-fil-A location on campus. A fixed amount of dining dollars is included in every meal plan to give you additional flexibility to eat what and where you want. Dining Dollars for all meal plans are distributed by semester, half in the fall and half in the spring. Unused Dining Dollars will carry over from fall to spring semester and will expire when the meal contract ends after finals week in May.

sections.section_title: How does my meal plan work?
sections.text:
Eating on-campus is easy with any Purdue Dining & Culinary Meal Plans. We have five all-you-care-to-eat dining courts, 4 On-the-Go locations, and 3 Quick Bite locations. Once you have a meal plan, your student I.D. works like a debit card. Swipe your card, tap your phone, and the meal swipe or dining dollars are automatically deducted from your meal plan account. Our meal plans have two components: Meal Swipe and Dining Dollars.
Meal Swipe: The number of swipes you have available each week is based on your selected meal plan. Meal swipes can be used in any of the five dining courts: Hillenbrand dining court, Earhart dining court, Windsor dining court, Wiley dining court, and Ford dining court, 4 On-the-Go locations: Earhart On-the-Go, Windsor On-the-Go, Ford On-the-Go, and Lawson On-the-Go, and 3 Quick Bite locations: 1 Bowl and Sushi Boss @ Meredith Hall, and Pete's Za @ Tarkington Hall. The cashier will deduct one meal from your plan upon entering these locations. You can also take your Meal to Go from our five dining courts, informing the cashier when you enter the dining court. Unused meal swipes will expire each Sunday and the meal plans will reset for the following week. Dining Dollars: This feature is accepted at all five dining courts, Chick-fil-A, and Purdue Food Co. locations. Dining Dollars work like a debit card; each time you purchase, the total is deducted from the balance in your account. Guests: Bringing your parents or a friend to a dining court?  Meal swipes are non-transferable (cannot be used for guests).  Dining dollars can be used to bring guests to the dining courts.  Dining Courts also accept Visa, MasterCard, and American Express. ResidentialDining/GuestMealPrices

sections.section_title: What happens if I find a transaction error in my account?
sections.text:
To view your dining transactions, including Meal Swipe and Dining Dollars balances, visit eAccounts . If you find an error on a transaction, please send an email to mealplans@purdue.edu within two days so we can investigate the issue and make any corrections needed. When sending your email, please be sure to include the following information:
Student’s name and university e-mail address. Description of the transaction in question and explain as clearly as possible the discrepancy. Transaction type (Meal Swipe or Dining Dollars) including the amount.

sections.section_title: How does dine-in versus carry-out in the dining courts work?
sections.text:
Dine-in:
Enjoy all-you-care-to-eat while inside the dining court. Only one piece of handheld fruit is allowed to be carried out. You may not leave the dining court with any additional food. If you would like to eat in the dining court and then get carry-out, you must exit the dining court, return to the end of the line, and re-enter to use another meal swipe to get a carry-out box.
Carry-out:
Students are allowed 1 carry-out per meal period (breakfast, lunch, and dinner). Unlimited meal plan holders are limited to 8 OTG!, Quick Bite, or dining court carry-out swipes per week. Students must notify the door checker that they are getting carry-out instead of dining in before they tap or swipe their PUID. Carry-out containers and to-go cups will be provided by the dining court. Carry-out container lids must be securely closed. No overloading of containers will be allowed. In addition to your carry-out container, you may carry out: 1 hot or cold beverage in a cup. (Please do not place other items inside beverage cup.) 1 whole piece of fruit or hand-held dessert. You may not dine in or sit in the dining court with a carry-out container. Violators will be reported and will face disciplinary action.

sections.section_title: How do I store my carry-out food safely?
sections.text:
You want to avoid allowing your food to stay between 40°F and 140°F degrees which is commonly referred to as the Temperature Danger Zone. Keeping food out of the danger zone will ensure it’s safe to eat by limiting the growth of bacteria. This range - between 40 °F and 140 °F - is where bacteria is the happiest and most comfortable, and where it can grow rapidly.
Four hours is the maximum time that ready-to-eat foods can stay in the temperature danger zone. After the 4 hour limit, foods must be thrown away.
Ways to avoid the Danger Zone:
Get what you need and eat what you get. Eat your carry-out food within two hours if you are not keeping it in a warmer or a refrigerator. Keep in mind that it is easier to keep cold food cold than to cool food off. Just because you put food into a refrigerator doesn’t mean it immediately out of the Danger Zone, especially in a hall room fridge. The time that your food takes to cool down counts towards the 2 hour limit. Consider visiting an OTG! Location. We cooled it down for you giving you more flexibility. Sites like the USDA have additional information on food safety guidelines. The more you know the better!

sections.section_title: What if my card doesn't work?
sections.text:
Email mealplans@purdue.edu for help. If after hours, you will be contacted the next business day.
For issues with Mobile ID, contact ID Card Operations at purdueidcardoffice@purdue.edu .

sections.section_title: Will Purdue be able to meet my special dietary needs?
sections.text:
We offer a wide variety of items in our dining courts to accommodate special dietary needs. Please visit the Food Allergies information page or the Special Dietary Needs information page for specific details about managing these diets.
Students are able to request a dietary accommodation if they do not feel they can successfully self-navigate in the dining courts. Students can begin this process by contacting the Disability Resource Center and disclosing their dietary needs and concerns.
Please contact our registered dietitian by emailing allergicboiler@purdue.edu if you have questions about dining on campus with a special diet.

sections.section_title: What forms of payment do the dining courts accept and how much does a meal cost for a guest?
sections.text: The dining courts accept Dining Dollars, Visa, MasterCard and American Express. For information on pricing for guests, please visit our Guest Meal Prices webpage .

We are always looking for students who would like to work in a fun, fast-paced environment. We offer flexible schedules and convenient locations. As an added bonus, you receive a free meal with each shift you work. You can contact any dining court directly or follow this link to fill out an application online.

What meal plans are available to students in Indianapolis ? Meal plans will be required for students residing in North Hall and available to all Purdue in Indianapolis students. Students with a meal plan can eat at Tower Dining or one of the Chartwells-managed locations across campus using meal swipes loaded onto their affiliate Crimson Card. Click here to view all meal plans provided by IUI dining for Purdue students in Indianapolis. Explore the dining options available with your meal plan at dineoncampus.com/iui . Do I have to live in University Residences housing options to have a meal plan ? Any student located in Indianapolis can sign up for a meal plan, even if they do not live in University Residences housing options. IUI dining offers meal plans to students who do not live on campus. Eat a variety of foods and regularly try new ones Build a colorful plate with different fruits and vegetables Be mindful of serving sizes Enjoy your favorite desserts and sugary foods in moderation All foods fit in a healthy diet and lifestyle

Purdue Dining & Culinary is dedicated to our work with students, staff, and guests who have food allergies by providing options in all Residential dining locations. Purdue Dining & Culinary is not in a position to guarantee a completely allergen-free environment. Rather, we are dedicated to minimizing the risk of exposure and we encourage all students and guests to disclose their needs and plan for effective response to possible emergencies.

Purdue Dining & Culinary Residential dining locations label all food products with the Top 9 allergens plus gluten and coconut to help individuals with food allergies make informed choices:
Coconut, Eggs, Fish, Gluten, Milk, Peanuts, Shellfish, Sesame, Soy, Tree Nuts, Wheat
Allergens are coded with icons that can be seen on our mobile app and Menus Online . Food items are labeled based on the ingredient list provided by the manufacturer, meaning we tag items as containing an allergen versus being free-from an allergen.
A  yellow caution icon signals that we are unable to provide nutrition information for that item. This is a “safety net”; it is our way of saying we would rather provide no information than provide wrong information. Any time an item being served does not match the recipe in our menu-management system, we use this caution icon to indicate no nutrition information is available. A dining court supervisor can provide allergen information for these items.
We occasionally serve items in the dining courts that are not published to the app. A dining court supervisor can provide allergen information for these items.

All students are highly encouraged to download the Purdue Mobile Menus app.
View all locations' menus in advance Navigate and determine which dining court and location best fits your needs View allergen tags, nutrition facts label, and full ingredient list Allergen filter Favorites feature
Menus online utilizes the same database and information as the mobile app but allows user to view the menu in a web browser.
Use the Ctrl+F function to search for items in the ingredient list that are not coded with an icon. Example: Non-top 9 allergens like celery or mustard

Purdue Dining & Culinary’s full-time employees are ServSafe and AllerTrain certified. Our staff are willing to answer questions you may have about food preparation, ingredients, cooking methods, or recipes. Students should ask to speak to the “supervisor on duty” if they have any specific questions or concerns when dining.

Several dining courts operate a specialty station dedicated to allergen-free food. Students with food allergies are not limited to only eating at these stations; they may find that the offerings at our other stations meet their needs.
Ford - Simple Servings Hillenbrand - SafeBytes Top 9 Free Wiley - Gluten Friendly
Simple Servings stations consist of a plant-based protein, starch, and hot vegetable. The offerings at this station/menu are free of all animal products, including eggs and milk.
SafeBytes is free of the Top 9 Allergens plus gluten. The food is staff-served to reduce the risk of cross contact. This station is separate from the main kitchen and service lines to avoid allergen exposure.
Additionally, Hillenbrand Dining Court does not serve any items containing peanuts or tree nuts.
The Gluten Friendly station at Wiley Dining Court serves a protein, starch, and vegetable made without gluten-containing ingredients. This station is self-serve and may be suitable for students with gluten sensitivities or preferences.
The dining courts also offer a variety of build-your-own stations, which are customizable to the needs and preferences of our students. Examples of custom entrees include pizza, pasta, stir-fry, salad, and deli sandwich. Be sure to ask a staff member if you require specialty ingredients like gluten-free pizza crust or pasta.

Some students with food allergies may require further assistance in our Residential dining operations. Take the following steps to request a dietary accommodation.
Contact our Registered Dietitian at allergicboiler@purdue.edu to connect with Purdue Dining & Culinary. Our dietitian will need to know what food allergies you have and what your typical diet is like. Connect with the Disability Resource Center . On the DRC website , click "Request Accommodations," and complete the embedded form. An Access Consultant will contact you to schedule a meeting and obtain medical documentation of your food allergy or medical condition from a physician.
The Disability Resource Center determines if you are a qualified student with a disability. Qualified students with a disability are eligible for dietary accommodations. Dining & Culinary’s Registered Dietitian determines appropriate and reasonable accommodations for these students in conjunction with the Disability Resource Center. Students will receive communication from the dietitian about implementing their accommodation.

Food Allergy Research & Education
Celiac Disease Foundation

Purdue Dining & Culinary Residential dining locations label food items as vegan and vegetarian using an icon. These icons can be seen on our mobile app and Menus Online . Our online resources can help students find vegan and vegetarian offerings at the Residential dining locations.
Disclaimer: We label our fried foods based on the ingredient list on the packaging. Certain cooking methods like deep-frying may alter an individual’s perception of whether or not an item is considered vegan or vegetarian. Please ask a dining employee about the preparation of your food item if you have concerns about how a food is labeled.

Several dining courts operate a specialty station dedicated to vegan and vegetarian food. Vegan and vegetarian students are not limited to only eating at these stations; they may find that the offerings at our other stations meet their needs.
Ford - Simple Servings Windsor - Vegetarian

Windsor Dining Court offers halal food at lunch and dinner. This includes a variety of halal proteins including beef, chicken, and fish. Windsor also has a large selection of plant-based protein options in its menu rotation.

Purdue Dining & Culinary does not have a certified kosher kitchen. However, Purdue Dining does offer Kosher Purple Diamond program, which offers Glatt Kosher, OU-certified pre-plated meals at Earhart and Ford Dining Courts for lunch and dinner. This program is available to current University Residences meal plan holders.
Students who would like to sign up should contact mealplans@purdue.edu , after which they will receive directions and additional program details. Meals are pre-plated, double-wrapped, and served dine-in only to ensure kosher standards.

Molly Puffenberger, MS, RD
mwinkele@purdue.edu

Purdue Dining & Culinary coordinates with several different classes throughout the academic year. Food Science 443 students, as part of their senior capstone project, developed a new snack item in conjunction with the Central Production Kitchen. Industrial Engineering 431 groups have worked in many locations to improve line queueing, production processes, and design layouts. Mechanical Engineering 463 students have converted discarded fruit and vegetable peels, egg shells, and coffee grounds from the dining courts into energy sources. Dining & Culinary has also worked with students in Industrial Engineering 386, Communication 114, Civil Engineering 398, English 106, Management 361, and the Krannert MBA program. Dining & Culinary is willing to work with any student groups on projects. For more information email Mary Jo Zeiser, Student Success Manager, at mjzeiser@purdue.edu .
Soybean Oil Research with Department of Food Science

“To nourish and inspire” remains Purdue Dining & Culinary’s mission not only while serving our campus community but as an academic partner in every pursuit. PD&C continues its lifelong commitment to develop our students as employees, leaders and professionals.
Purdue Dining and Culinary offers semester long, comprehensive internship opportunities for a variety of academic disciplines. Our mission is to provide an internship that combines practical work experience with targeted benchmarks throughout the semester, allowing our students to leave with skills they will carry with them well past graduation.
Partnering with multiple colleges across campus allows students the opportunity to meet internship requirements for their program of study while still completing required classwork. PD&C has worked with students in the following classes; Nutrition 350, Food & Nutrition in Business 375, Food Science 491, Psychology 492, Retail Management 300, HTM 202 and 302. PD&C has the ability to work with any student on cultivating an internship experience to fit their needs.
For more information on internship opportunities, contact us:
Mary Jo Zeiser Senior Student Success Manager, Programs mjzeiser@purdue.edu
Gretchen Jewell Associate Director of Auxiliary Dining Services jewellg@purdue.edu

Purdue Dining & Culinary offers a ten-week Food Service and Nutrition Summer Internship. This internship is open to all undergraduate students in a dietetics program. The internship offers a competitive hourly salary, a paid meal plan, and free housing in our residence halls.
The curriculum will include practical instruction in our residential dining court that covers the front of the house and the back of the house. This instruction will prepare the intern to act as a fully functional member of the dining court team by week nine. Interns will complete a summer-long recipe development project as well as a group project creating a themed dessert station.
In addition to the practical instruction, the internship will provide career-building experiences. Networking opportunities from industry professionals will be available on a weekly basis via virtual meetings or in-person luncheons. On-site tours of food manufacturers, vendors and local restaurants will enhance the intern’s overall experience.
The co-chairs of the internship will be actively involved allowing the interns to reflect and provide feedback throughout the internship.
Applications will be accepted starting January 21, 2025 and will close February 21, 2025. Interviews will take place March 10 - 12, 2025. Applicants accepted to the internship will be notified no later than March 28, 2025. (Please note: Any application that is not complete by the closing date will not be considered for an interview.)

In order to be considered for this internship, applicants must submit: Resume Personal statement Two letters of recommendation Please choose references from two of the three following categories Academic Employment Personal/character Use the following link to upload all documents: Internship Application A representative from Purdue Dining & Culinary will provide references with a separate link via email to upload the letter of recommendation Top candidates will receive a phone interview

Molly Winkeler, MS, RD, LD PD&C Registered Dietitian PD&C Summer Intern Chair mwinkele@purdue.edu
Kelsey Collier Dining Court Supervisor PD&C Summer Intern Co-Chair colliekm@purdue.edu

Looking for a paycheck, real-world work experience, and a flexible schedule that fits your busy life? Purdue Dining & Culinary offers students all of these things and more, boosting your bank account while giving you valuable experience that employers are looking for after graduation.
Earn a paycheck without having to leave campus
Decide how many working hours are comfortable for you
Opportunities for advancement
Students Apply Here!

Jumpstart is a student employment program for undergraduate students that takes place before Boiler Gold Rush. Accepted students will move in seven days prior to BGR to begin the Jumpstart program. Students receive tips from mentors on how to balance work and classes, get to know our campus, make new friends, and learn how to do their job within Purdue Dining & Culinary!
Participating students will be eligible to receive a refund on BGR fees if they work five shifts during BGR and continue working until September 15th. Purdue Dining & Culinary will pay the early arrival cost, provide meals every shift and help you move in!
Jumpstart is a fantastic experience for anyone considering student employment at Purdue, but don't take our word for it! Read what our students have said about the experience:
Applications open in June. If you have any questions, please email us at dining-PDCHiring@purdue.edu

Looking for more than your average food service job?
From cook to dishwasher, the opportunities available across our units are as diverse as our work force. Purdue Dining & Culinary can offer you a great job fit and room to grow in your career path.
Development & Advancement Opportunities Heath Insurance & Tuition Reduction Paid Time Off & 10 Vacation Days in Your First Year
Full-time and part-time, positions with benefits are available in many units. Hourly positions include production coordinators, grill cooks, bakers, prep cooks, team leads, clerks, drivers, warehouse and customer service associates.
Our dining court team provides the taste and feeling of home for student residents in five locations. Our quick bite and on-the-go locations provide quick options for students who don't have time to stop and sit down for a meal.
Working behind the scenes to keep Purdue Dining & Culinary running smoothly, the Central Culinary and Strategic Support team manage purchasing functions, deliver food across campus, and support our menu management software.
Leading these teams in salaried positions, are supervisors, executive sous chefs, supervisors, purchasing agents, managers and administrators.
Ready to apply? Of course you are! Click on the button below to view available positions.
Apply Today!

The Above and Beyond the Call of Duty Award was established in 2008 to recognize student employees in Purdue Dining & Culinary and University Residences whose actions make a difference at the university and how they contribute to excellence within these two departments. It is awarded four times throughout the academic year and is a $300 award that was included in each of these student’s paychecks.

sections.section_title: NSEA Student Employee of the Year Award
sections.text:
Each spring the National Student Employment Association coordinates a selection process and chooses the National Student Employee of the Year to recognize the outstanding contributions and achievements of students who work while attending college.
Winners: 2023-2024: Stephanie Colby from Hillenbrand Dining Court, nominated by Neal Williamson 2022-2023: Jade Frye from 1 Bowl, nominated by Colleen Norman 2020-2021: Michelle Rybicki from Windsor Dining Court, nominated by Madison Tarter 2019-2020: Rachel Nellett from Windsor Dining Court, nominated by Sierra Morin 2018-2019: Robert Hughes from Port, nominated by Whitney Wood 2017-2018: Joe Youngkeit from Port, nominated by Whitney Wood 2016-2017: Breille Starost from LaSalsa, nominated by Mary Jane Hoover 2015-2016: Avery Sameshima from Harrison Grillé, nominated by Arline Sprau 2014-2015: Stephanie Buehler from Athletic Dining Room, nominated by Carolyn Shultz 2013-2014: Ashley Culross from Starbucks, nominated by Peggy Hunley

sections.section_title: William H. “Bill” Rumple Residence Dining Hall Head Captain Award
sections.text:
This award was established in recognition of William and Tim Rumple. Both of these men provided outstanding service to University Residences Dining Services as former Head Waiters at Fowler Court and Cary Quadrangle. This award is given annually to a Student Manager or Student Coordinator in Residential Dining Services who has achieved distinction in leadership while employed for Dining Services.
Winners: 2024-2025: Caleb Blanton from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Scott Sarjent 2023-2024: Alexandria Bedard from Lawson OTG, nominated by Marita Jarvis 2022-2023: Crosley Stanley from Ford Dining Court, nominated by Charlotte Kalish 2020-2021: Kendall Green from Lawson OTG, nominated by Laurie Kender 2019-2020: Noor Elmahdi from Ford Dining Court, nominated by Charlotte Kalish 2018-2019: Rachel Nellett from Windsor Dining Court, nominated by Sierra Morin 2017-2018: Katlynn-Ann Douglass from Wiley Dining Court, nominated by Alicia Oliver 2016-2017: Cameron Scott from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill 2015-2016: Allexys Woodward from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill 2014-2015: Kelsey Gehlhausen from Harrison Grillé, nominated by Judy Sharkey 2013-2014: Daniel Misch from Harrison Grillé, recommended by Erik Kurdelak

sections.section_title: Sarah Johnson Oustanding Student Employee of the Year Award
sections.text:
The Sarah Johnson Outstanding Student Employee of the Year Award Fund was established to recognize the many contributions of Sarah Johnson, former director of Residential Dining for University Residences. Sarah dedicated over 37 years to students at Purdue University and National Association of Colleges & University Food Services (NACUFS). The Outstanding Student Employee of the Year award is selected on reliability, quality of work, initiative, teamwork and contribution to Purdue Dining & Culinary.
Winners: 2024-2025: Britney Mitchell from Central Hiring, nominated by Mary Jo Zeiser 2023-2024: Jarod Ball from Wiley Dining Court, nominated by Jessica Scott 2022-2023: Jillian Pruss from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Eric Coates 2020-2021: Alley Avery from Windsor Dining Court, nominated by Maddie Tarter 2019-2020: Rachel Nellett from Windsor Dining Court, nominated by Sierra Morin 2018-2019: Josh Leaver from Ford Dining Court, nominated by Charlotte Kalish 2017-2018: Marquette Minner from Windsor Dining Court, nominated by Stephanie Packer 2016-2017: Robert Komis from Student Success, nominated by Mary Jo Zeiser 2015-2016: Andrea Hallmen from Ford Dining Court, nominated by Ashley Sargent 2014-2015: Chris Lucas from Athletic Dining Room, nominated by Mary Jo Zeiser 2013-2014: Bradley Knoblock from Ford Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill

This award recognizes a student whose work exemplifies the standards of excellence in quality and service for which Mrs. Wiltshire strove toward during her 25 years of service to the residence halls and Purdue University. Wiltshire Work Ethic Award is awarded to an Earhart student employee that has worked 2 semesters or longer, has a 2.5 GPA, and shows consistent and excellent work ethic.
Winners: 2024-2025: Riley Haan from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Scott Sarjent 2023-2024: Jacob Patty from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Scott Sarjent 2020-2021: Ross Reyes from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Jon Newberry 2019-2020: Sarah Hart from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Jon Newberry 2018-2019: Zowie Lemons from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill 2017-2018: Khameron Allen from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill 2016-2017: Janessa King from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill 2015-2016: Heidi Jones from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill 2014-2015: Darci Simmons from Earhart Dining Court, nominated by Kathy Vill
